Buying low is always a good idea when you are investing, regardless of whether the future is bullish or bearish.

Above all, make sure you are buying investments that you are comfortable holding through a downswing. If you're going to bail and sell something because it is down 50%, then you probably shouldn't be buying it today.

Let's talk about what I'm buying.

Fixed-Income

Preferreds

I've been pounding the table on fixed-income for three years. The largest part of my fixed-income portfolio is preferred equity. Preferred equity provides a predetermined dividend that is senior to the common but junior to the debt. This means that in order to pay a common dividend, the company has to be current on its preferred dividends. However, it is equity, and it is subordinate to debt, which means that its credit risk is a bit higher.

The result is that investors can get a higher yield from preferred equity than from debt, while getting a predetermined and more stable dividend payment than you get from the common equity. Investors want to make sure that they are buying preferred shares with companies that are good credit risks.

Our strategy is heavily focused on preferred outside of banks and insurance companies. We don't have anything against them, but the preferred for banks and insurance companies tends to be a more crowded trade with fewer good deals. Preferred or baby bonds for REITs (Real Estate Investment Trusts), BDCs (Business Development Companies), CEFs (Closed-End Funds), and other RICs (Regulated Investment Companies) often have higher yields while taking on what we view as an acceptable credit risk.

Virtus InfraCap US Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA) is an ETF that focuses on preferred stocks and holds many of the preferred stocks we invest in at HDO. It has materially outperformed peer funds that follow the index and are heavily invested in bank and insurance preferred.

PFFA is a great investment option; however, it is an ETF. That means it will adjust its dividend to reflect its taxable income. In 2020, we saw preferred prices crash, and PFFA cut its distribution, even though most preferred continued paying their dividends. PFFA and other preferred funds will provide you with the economic return of preferred equities, but they will not necessarily provide you with the predictable recurring cash flow.

I own both. I hold PFFA for the economic return and the leveraged exposure to preferred equities. I also hold a large portfolio of individual preferred equities that pay me a predetermined dividend and will have more cash flow stability in the event of a crash like COVID-19.

If you aren't actively withdrawing and you don't plan on withdrawing cash, then PFFA, combined with some other funds for diversification, might be a more efficient way to have broad exposure to the asset class. However, if you are relying on income in your portfolio, then you definitely want to have significant holdings in fixed-income assets with a combination of preferred and bonds.

Bonds

Like preferred, bonds are fixed-income, but they pay interest, and they are a legal obligation. This means that if a borrower defaults, the bondholders can sue, and usually the company ends up going to bankruptcy court. As a result, bonds are usually of a lower credit risk.

But be aware, this relative safety can vary company to company. Some companies like Chimera Investment Corporation (CIM) have a very thin difference between their baby bonds and preferred. The majority of their debt is secured and senior to their baby bonds. As a result, in a bankruptcy situation, odds are high that the baby bonds and preferred would have a materially similar (and bad) recovery. We aren't buying CIM bonds because we believe that they would recover in bankruptcy; we buy them because we believe that bankruptcy is a remote risk for CIM. The attraction of the bonds over the preferred is the maturity date.

CIM's preferred are perpetual, which means that their yield will always reflect whatever the market believes is fair to reflect the current credit risk. Bonds mature on a specific date. As that date approaches, the bond's price will approach par unless bankruptcy is imminent because the bond will be redeemed at par. As a result, bonds that are near maturity will have prices that trend closer to par than bonds with more distant maturities. And bonds will have prices that are stickier than preferred. The bond will repay par at a known point in the future (barring bankruptcy), whereas the perpetual preferred can remain outstanding forever. So, there is no reason for it to "have to" return to par.

We recommend setting up a maturity ladder (a concept we have many times on Seeking Alpha), so that you have bonds that are maturing every year. This will provide your portfolio with a constant flow of maturities. The bonds closest to maturity will tend to stay closer to par, and in a crash, you will receive cash from maturities, or you can sell bonds that are close to maturity near par to take advantage of other buying opportunities.

Agency mREITs

We've discussed agency mREITs (mortgage REITs) as a countercyclical option that usually performs well during recessions. Annaly Capital Management (NLY) is the oldest mREIT to follow the agency MBS strategy. Here is how it did in 2000-2002:

And here is 2007-2009:

In both cases, the combination of an oil price spike and a faltering economy caused outsized returns for NLY.

The reason this happens is that NLY invests in agency MBS, an asset class that has minimal credit risk. The agencies, Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac buy mortgages and then put a guarantee on them. If the borrower defaults, the agency buys back the mortgage at par. As a result, agency MBS does well when investors are fleeing to safety.

This makes NLY and its peers a powerful option to protect your portfolio from a declining economy. It is during recessions that they are raising their dividends the most, and during bull markets when they are reducing their dividends.

Tangible Businesses

Infrastructure, REITs, MLPs (Master Limited Partnership), and other businesses that own tangible and essential infrastructure that generate cash flows, make up a huge portion of our stock picks. The reason is that these cash cows produce the dividends that achieve our portfolio goals and tend to have stable cash flow in a wide variety of economic conditions.

We are especially bullish on these today because we see valuations as relatively low for most "Value" stocks, and these are quintessential Value stocks. They are companies that are valued based on their current cash flow.

There's downside risk to these types of investments in terms of price, and there could be risk if their sector is the center of a recession. In 2008, REITs weren't the best option for cash flow stability. In 2001, they were. We don't believe that REITs, oil, or infrastructure are likely to be materially weak during the next recession, but we diversify because we don't know the future.

We believe that the current set-up most closely resembles the Dot-com Bust. Here is how one of our REIT funds, Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI), performed from 2002-2004:

Credit Risk

The portion of our portfolio that is currently underperforming the most is credit risk. While defaults objectively remain low and we haven't seen major credit losses in our picks, sentiment has definitely turned negative toward the types of credit holdings we have exposure to – especially CLO (Collateralized Loan Obligation) equity and BDCs.

We've discussed in other articles why we believe the negative sentiment is inaccurate and is overstating the risks, but it has a real and meaningful impact on share prices. In the near-term, we expect sentiment to remain negative. If there is a positive shift in the overall market from fear to greed, say the Iranian war ends with a successful peace deal, and the markets turn euphoric, we could see a "risk on" move, and these holdings would experience a rapid recovery.

Otherwise, it will come down to the numbers. The actual default rate will be X%, and to the extent that it is less than the market expects, it will be positive for prices and dividends. And if it is more than the market is currently expecting, that will be negative for prices and dividends.

Valuations are very low for some credit risk options, and it's an interesting opportunity to add if you have already addressed the stability and safety of your portfolio with the holdings discussed above.

Conclusion

We can't predict the future, but we can construct a portfolio that is ready for it. The portfolio discussed above is one that is biased toward safety if there is a recession. It is heavy in fixed-income, has a maturity ladder, has countercyclical holdings, and investments in cash cows. Those are all investments that we would expect to outperform if a bear market occurs. They might not be immune to price swings, but they will produce ample dividends, providing us with cash for as long as the bear market persists.

Once that base is in place, we view credit risk as a positive risk/reward. By that, we mean higher risk, but a very high potential reward, making the risk worthwhile. Credit risk investments is where we could see very large returns if the market's current bearishness towards CLOs and BDCs wanes.

Put together, we have a portfolio that we believe is optimized to face the challenging investment environment we are in today.

data.title: Cisco Remains A Buy As AI Tailwinds Broaden
data.description:
As you can see in the chart below, I have changed my view on Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO) quite a few times in the past year or so. Nonetheless, in my most recent article, I upgraded the stock to a buy rating citing AI infrastructure momentum acceleration and undervaluation relative to the IT sector. Since then, there has been some mild gains which isn't bad considering the risk-off environment recently. While the company reported earnings quite a while back, their next batch of results aren't due until mid next month and so today I have decided to provide a fresh perspective and an update for the investment thesis.

Below, it is shown that Cisco's fundamentals are quite solid despite some mixed numbers. Top line growth accelerated and operating margin held largely steady. Looking forward, guidance is also encouraging and despite some near term risks like the Middle East conflict, AI tailwinds are broadening for the company. Their expertise in wireless connectivity and cybersecurity are set to pay off. With the forward P/E still at a discount to the sector, a reiteration of my buy rating is justified in my view.

Growth Looks Solid

We'll start today with a look at their FY2026 Q2 top line numbers. In the quarter, the company as a whole generated revenues of $15.35 billion, up 9.71% YoY. While this isn't the most exciting of growth rates, you can see above that this was an acceleration from the previous quarter and that relative to the past few years, Cisco is performing in a reasonably strong manner right now. The company also managed to breeze past expectations as they beat by over $225 million to show outperformance. Business activity seemed solid.

Some investors may be wondering why growth isn't that high despite their strong AI momentum. Well, while news coverage is now mainly focussed on their AI business, you can see in the above table that Cisco in reality is a quite diversified business with solutions spanning security to observability. You can also see that even though networking saw robust growth, areas like services were somewhat of a drag to their performance. Nonetheless, networking is their most important business now and the 21% of growth for Q2 was a notable acceleration from Q1's 15% and so there are indeed signs that AI continues to provide meaningful tailwinds for their business activity.

Is Demand Softening?

When looking at their RPO and ARR results, there may be some concern that demand is softening. In just a second we'll talk about their order stats but as you can see above, Product RPO and Services RPO growth slowed from the previous quarter to 8% and 2%, respectively. For ARR, there was also deterioration in both Product and Services with the latter showing YoY declines. You can also see that these numbers are quite a contrast to the year ago quarter's numbers and so there are signs that momentum for Cisco is weakening.

While RPO and ARR stats are potentially worrying, there are also still signs that their AI business is solid. In Q2, they saw 18% of product order growth which is a speedup from 13% in the previous quarter. For networking orders in particular they saw a 20% jump YoY. This is just a hair lower than the networking revenue growth rate that was reported in the quarter. Lastly, AI infrastructure orders from hyperscalers totalled $2.1 billion in the quarter and that is a massive QoQ increase from $1.3 billion in Q1. Therefore, while there may potentially be some softness in other areas of their business, AI tailwinds seem set to continue in my view.

Memory Costs Are A Risk

As mentioned by a Seeking Alpha article back in February, memory costs are a concern for Cisco's profitability. With higher hardware mix also being a factor, gross margin saw contraction from 68.7% to 67.5% YoY for the company as a whole. Cisco was able to control their expenses in an impressive manner, however.

OPEX dropped by 110 basis points YoY as a percentage of revenues and that indicates an increasingly well run business. That was able to offset most of that gross margin weakness but ultimately operating margin saw some modest contraction.

On the bottom line, there was growth acceleration from the previous quarter and they also edged past EPS expectations. Still, moving forward investors would be wise to keep an eye on their gross margin as it is still to be seen whether they will effectively raise product prices to offset memory cost headwinds.

Full Year Outlook Raised

Cisco provided Q3 revenue guidance that was above consensus and EPS guidance that was in-line. However, let's focus on the annual outlook for a better sense of where things are at in the grand scheme of things. After beating on the top and bottom lines for Q2, Cisco raised their annual guidance.

For revenue, at the new midpoint, they are implying annual growth of around 8%. While that doesn't seem overly exciting, note that growth in 2025 was just 5% and so in fact there are signs that business activity will strengthen in a meaningful manner moving forward.

As for EPS, the new midpoint would represent an approximately 9% increase annually. Since that outpaces the projected top line growth rate slightly, it likely means that strong OPEX control will again offset the potential gross margin weakness. Memory costs are still a risk moving forward but as a whole, profitability is expected to remain resilient.

The Value Of AI

Before moving on to a discussion of some recent events, it is worth mentioning the findings in Cisco's recent State of Industrial AI Report as it shows that AI is providing real value. AI bears question the payoff from investments but Cisco's findings show that the opportunity at hand is large. In a recent press release, it is stated that

The double-blind global study surveyed more than 1,000 operational technology (OT) decision‑makers across 19 countries and 21 industrial sectors. The findings show that AI is now delivering measurable operational benefits in use cases such as process automation, automated quality inspection, predictive maintenance, logistics, and energy forecasting. However, many organizations are increasingly constrained by readiness gaps in networking infrastructure, cybersecurity, and IT/OT operating models as AI shifts into real‑time, production‑grade use in physical environments.

At this point, it seems that Cisco is well positioned to step into these readiness gaps. The company obviously offers networking solutions but note that they also specialize in areas such as security. Therefore, I believe their assessment of the industry shows that their growth runway moving forward is solid.

Recent Opportunities & Risks

Back in early March it was reported that Nvidia (NVDA) and several telecom companies were committing to building the 6G network on AI-native, open, and secure platforms. Cisco is part of this initiative and so this is another reminder that the company's reach is far beyond just data center products. By now, it is becoming increasingly obvious that 6G will be crucial for physical AI and I believe Cisco's expertise in wireless connectivity positions the company well for this wave. Therefore, AI tailwinds seem set to broaden moving forward.

Going back to security solutions again, Cisco is in position to see tailwinds in this area as well. Just days ago, Anthropic announced Project Glasswing. As Brandon Evans of Seeking Alpha writes, this is

a cybersecurity initiative that will be built with the capabilities of its most powerful model, Claude Mythos Preview, with help from the most prolific software and security providers.

One of Anthropic's partners for this initiative is Cisco and so the wide reach of the company's offerings is again paying off for them. Their participation in a direction that may fundamentally change the trajectory of cybersecurity moving forward again shows that tailwinds are broadening.

In addition to the memory cost risks highlighted earlier, the conflict in the Middle East could be a threat to their operations and infrastructure in the region. On April 1st, it was reported that Iran has threatened a list of tech companies. Sure enough, Cisco was included on that target list. While long term effects may be limited, in the near term, the company has likely experienced disruption in the region and that may impact their performance moving forward.

Valuation Still At A Discount

Cisco's forward P/E ratio has been on a relatively steady climb in recent times and the current reading of 19.63 is quite near multiyear highs. However, as of this writing, Seeking Alpha shows that their non-GAAP forward P/E is still at a ~9% discount to the IT sector. I believe this shows that Cisco stock remains attractive now.

In today's article, there were some mixed numbers discussed but as a whole, Cisco's fundamentals and outlook are still robust in my view. For example, RPO and ARR numbers were a bit shaky but strong order growth shows that AI demand remains solid. Furthermore, while gross margin is a risk, strong OPEX control in Q2 and raised full year EPS guidance show that profitability is expected to remain resilient. Lastly, while there are other near term risks like the Middle East war, the company's expertise in wireless connectivity and cybersecurity is paying off as long term opportunities arise.

Cisco's fundamentals aren't perfect and they don't need to be at this stage. AI tailwinds are set to continue in a strong manner and in fact tailwinds are set to broaden. With the forward P/E ratio still at a discount to the sector, I believe Cisco's current stock price offers a favorable risk/reward setup.

Conclusion

Cisco stock has seen just modest gains since my previous buy rating but as stated at the beginning, given the risk-off state in the markets, this resilience should not be complained about. After today's analysis, I have decided to reiterate my buy call. Things like the gross margin weakness and Iran threats have weighed on sentiment a bit but make no mistake, Cisco is well positioned to take advantage of the opportunities at hand. As stated above, tailwinds are set to broaden and with the valuation still being attractive, now isn't a bad time to build a position in this AI leader.
